No Poverty.reason: The course syllabus contains no explicit or implicit evidence related to poverty eradication or its specific targets.

Zero Hunger.reason: There is no mention of hunger, food security, agriculture, or related issues in the course description or objectives.

Good Health and Well-being.reason: The course content does not address health, well-being, disease prevention, or healthcare services.

Quality Education.reason: As a university course, it inherently contributes to quality tertiary education (Target 4.3) and develops professional skills for technology governance and policy research (Target 4.4).
evidence: Understand and synthesize contemporary legal and policy challenges
Independently design and complete a policy research project
Communicate policy ideas effectively through writing

Gender Equality.reason: The syllabus does not contain any specific references or implications related to gender equality or women's empowerment.

Clean Water and Sanitation.reason: No evidence in the course description or topics relates to water resources, sanitation, or water quality management.

Affordable and Clean Energy.reason: The course does not discuss energy access, renewable energy, or energy efficiency in any context.

Decent Work and Economic Growth.reason: The course directly addresses technological innovation and the semiconductor industry's role in economic productivity and competitiveness, aligning with Target 8.2.
evidence: Technological innovation is profoundly reshaping both economic and military landscapes
breakthroughs in semiconductor technology not only determine industrial competitiveness

Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure.reason: The course's central theme is the semiconductor industry, technological innovation, and industrial strategies, directly fulfilling Target 9.5 on upgrading technological capabilities and innovation.
evidence: Semiconductor Industry in the Era of Techno-Geopolitics
The evolution of semiconductor technology itself lies at the heart of this contest
forging new governance frameworks and industrial strategies

Reduced Inequalities.reason: The course discusses Taiwan's shift from 'periphery' to 'core' in global supply chains, tangentially relating to economic inequalities between nations (Target 10.6).
evidence: transition from the “periphery” to the “core” of the global supply chain

Sustainable Cities and Communities.reason: The course content does not involve urban development, sustainable communities, or related infrastructure.

Responsible Consumption and Production.reason: While supply chains are mentioned, the focus is geopolitical restructuring, not sustainable consumption, waste reduction, or resource efficiency.

Climate Action.reason: There is no discussion of climate change, its impacts, mitigation, or adaptation strategies in the syllabus.

Life Below Water.reason: The course content does not relate to marine ecosystems, ocean health, or sustainable fisheries.

Life on Land.reason: No evidence in the syllabus pertains to terrestrial ecosystems, biodiversity, deforestation, or land degradation.

Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions.reason: The course extensively covers national regulations, export controls, and governance frameworks, directly addressing effective institutions and rule of law for technology (Targets 16.3, 16.6).
evidence: security-related export control reforms, foreign investment reviews

Partnerships for the Goals.reason: The course analyzes international competition, cooperation, and policy coordination among nations on technology, aligning with global partnerships and policy coherence (Targets 17.6, 17.14).
evidence: different national perspectives—including those of Taiwan, the United States, Japan, and Europe
